mainmatter.p: When Forester encounters an undefined function, it automatically passes through to the XML output. For instance, if the function \myfun is not defined, then the Forester code \myfun{hello} will render to the XML code <myfun>hello</myfun>. The Forester 2.3 extends this fall-through behavior to support XML attributes.
In particular, if you write \myfun{key1}{value1}{key2}{value2}{hello}, Forester will render the following XML code:
Thanks to Masanori Ogino for suggesting this feature, and welcome to the Forester community!.
Armaël Guéneau, Riley Shahar, and Jon Sterling contributed code to this major release. Thanks also to Masanori Ogino for feature requests that made it into this release.

This release adds some new features to the command line interface. The original behavior of the forester command is now obtained by calling forester build. Please browse forester --help for more information.

frontmatter.title: Create new trees from the command line

mainmatter.p: A new command has been added in Forester 2.3 to simplify the creation of new trees with the correct base-36 identifier. If your personal namespace prefix is xxx, you may run the following command to create a new tree in the trees directory:
If the last tree in this directory was xxx-0039, a new tree will be generated at xxx-003A. The new tree will be populated with a correct \date declaration.

frontmatter.title: Tree title completion for tool support

mainmatter.p: A new forester complete command has been added to search for trees by their title; the purpose of this command is to facilitate external tool support, such as this proof-of-concept completion engine for neovim. Please browse forester complete --help for more information about this command.
Current limitations: the command is currently case sensitive and only searches prefixes; in the future, it would be desirable to support more flexible fuzzy-search.
Thanks to Riley Shahar for implementing this feature.

When Forester encounters an undefined function, it automatically passes through to the XML output. For instance, if the function \myfun is not defined, then the Forester code \myfun{hello} will render to the XML code <myfun>hello</myfun>. The Forester 2.3 extends this fall-through behavior to support XML attributes.
In particular, if you write \myfun{key1}{value1}{key2}{value2}{hello}, Forester will render the following XML code:
Thanks to Masanori Ogino for suggesting this feature, and welcome to the Forester community!.
In addition to the major changes above, the following minor changes are included in this release:

ol.li: A title attribute attribute has been added to the XML output of internal links, in order to facilitate tooltips and other affordances in rendered forests. Thanks to Armaël Guéneau for implementing this feature.
Subdirectories of input directories will now be traversed automatically; note that the tree address model remains flat, and subdirectories are present only for convenience.
Added a nicer command line interface with --help documentation.
I have migrated much of the system code to use the experimental Eio library for improved portability.
The example forest has been removed from the main repository, and moved into a separate template repository.

mainmatter.p: Armaël Guéneau, Riley Shahar, and Jon Sterling contributed code to this major release. Thanks also to Masanori Ogino for feature requests that made it into this release.

This release adds some new features to the command line interface. The original behavior of the forester command is now obtained by calling forester build. Please browse forester --help for more information.

frontmatter.title: Create new trees from the command line

mainmatter.p: A new command has been added in Forester 2.3 to simplify the creation of new trees with the correct base-36 identifier. If your personal namespace prefix is xxx, you may run the following command to create a new tree in the trees directory:
If the last tree in this directory was xxx-0039, a new tree will be generated at xxx-003A. The new tree will be populated with a correct \date declaration.

frontmatter.title: Tree title completion for tool support

mainmatter.p: A new forester complete command has been added to search for trees by their title; the purpose of this command is to facilitate external tool support, such as this proof-of-concept completion engine for neovim. Please browse forester complete --help for more information about this command.
Current limitations: the command is currently case sensitive and only searches prefixes; in the future, it would be desirable to support more flexible fuzzy-search.
Thanks to Riley Shahar for implementing this feature.

When Forester encounters an undefined function, it automatically passes through to the XML output. For instance, if the function \myfun is not defined, then the Forester code \myfun{hello} will render to the XML code <myfun>hello</myfun>. The Forester 2.3 extends this fall-through behavior to support XML attributes.
In particular, if you write \myfun{key1}{value1}{key2}{value2}{hello}, Forester will render the following XML code:
Thanks to Masanori Ogino for suggesting this feature, and welcome to the Forester community!.
In addition to the major changes above, the following minor changes are included in this release:

ol.li: A title attribute attribute has been added to the XML output of internal links, in order to facilitate tooltips and other affordances in rendered forests. Thanks to Armaël Guéneau for implementing this feature.
Subdirectories of input directories will now be traversed automatically; note that the tree address model remains flat, and subdirectories are present only for convenience.
Added a nicer command line interface with --help documentation.
I have migrated much of the system code to use the experimental Eio library for improved portability.
The example forest has been removed from the main repository, and moved into a separate template repository.

mainmatter.p: A tool for tending forests of evergreen notes.

link.title: Build your own Stacks Project in 10 minutes

td.link: How to create your own Stacks Project in 10 minutes

mainmatter.p: This is a breaking release for the XML format.

ol.li: Taxa used to be attributes of tree, but they are now child elements of tree/frontmatter. Therefore, what previously was written as <tree taxon="lemma"> <frontmatter> <!-- ... --> </frontmatter> <!-- ... --> </tree> is now written as follows: <tree> <frontmatter> <taxon>lemma</taxon> <!-- ... --> </frontmatter> <!-- ... --> </tree>

li.p: Dates are now stored in machine-readable format. For example, what used to be written <date>August 16, 2023</date> is now written as follows:
It is the responsibility of the XSLT stylesheet to format dates appropriately. Thanks to Masanori Ogino for suggesting this change.

ol.li: All identifiers in the XML format have been regularized to use snake-case; for instance, sourcePath is now source-path and show_heading is now show-heading.

mainmatter.p: Armaël Guéneau, Riley Shahar, and Jon Sterling contributed code to this major release. Thanks also to Masanori Ogino for feature requests that made it into this release.

This release adds some new features to the command line interface. The original behavior of the forester command is now obtained by calling forester build. Please browse forester --help for more information.

frontmatter.title: Create new trees from the command line

mainmatter.p: A new command has been added in Forester 2.3 to simplify the creation of new trees with the correct base-36 identifier. If your personal namespace prefix is xxx, you may run the following command to create a new tree in the trees directory:
If the last tree in this directory was xxx-0039, a new tree will be generated at xxx-003A. The new tree will be populated with a correct \date declaration.

frontmatter.title: Tree title completion for tool support

mainmatter.p: A new forester complete command has been added to search for trees by their title; the purpose of this command is to facilitate external tool support, such as this proof-of-concept completion engine for neovim. Please browse forester complete --help for more information about this command.
Current limitations: the command is currently case sensitive and only searches prefixes; in the future, it would be desirable to support more flexible fuzzy-search.
Thanks to Riley Shahar for implementing this feature.

When Forester encounters an undefined function, it automatically passes through to the XML output. For instance, if the function \myfun is not defined, then the Forester code \myfun{hello} will render to the XML code <myfun>hello</myfun>. The Forester 2.3 extends this fall-through behavior to support XML attributes.
In particular, if you write \myfun{key1}{value1}{key2}{value2}{hello}, Forester will render the following XML code:
Thanks to Masanori Ogino for suggesting this feature, and welcome to the Forester community!.
In addition to the major changes above, the following minor changes are included in this release:

ol.li: A title attribute attribute has been added to the XML output of internal links, in order to facilitate tooltips and other affordances in rendered forests. Thanks to Armaël Guéneau for implementing this feature.
Subdirectories of input directories will now be traversed automatically; note that the tree address model remains flat, and subdirectories are present only for convenience.
Added a nicer command line interface with --help documentation.
I have migrated much of the system code to use the experimental Eio library for improved portability.
The example forest has been removed from the main repository, and moved into a separate template repository.

mainmatter.p: This release replaces the old \transclude*, \transclude@ commands with a more flexible transclusion configuration mechanism using the support for fluid binding introduced in Forester 2.1. A basic transclusion with the default options is done in the same way as before:
The behavior of transclusion is, however, configurable by several globally allocated fluid bindings as depicted in the table below.

tr.td: To override the tree's title during transclusion.
Show the tree in the table of contents.
Whether the tree is expanded or not.
If set to false, the tree's contents will be inlined.
Whether the tree is displayed with a number or not, transitively on subtrees.

mainmatter.p: It is possible to achieve undesirable results by combining these options in undesirable ways; it is up to you to use them correctly.
The following code produces an unnumbered transclusion that nonetheless appears in the table of context, with an overridden title.
This release adds support for generating subsections from transclusions obtained by a query the forest; this can be used to produce topical bibliographies.
The following code will generate a subsection entitled “featured talks” containing the appropriate items as collapsed subtrees:
Under the hood, a query node is an “anonymous transclusion” and can therefore be configured using the same options as other transclusions.
A minor change is that the assets directly does not need to be flat anymore.
As always, existing forests can be upgraded by copying forest.xsl and style.css into their assets directory.
This is a major release that breaks compatibility of the XML format for trees; existing forests can be upgraded by copying forest.xsl and style.css into their assets directory.

ul.li: Development mode and edit button. A --dev option has been added to the forester executable; when this option is enabled, absolute paths will be embedded into tree metadata and an edit button is added to the top of the command palette.

Your own TeX preamble. There is no longer a hardcoded LaTeX preamble used for compiling graphics; instead, you can store your own .sty files in the assets directory and import them locally in a specific tree using the new \texpackage{...} frontmatter command.

Fluid binding constructs. Macros in the Forester language have functional semantics, which means that they capture their environment at the time of definition; this differs from the stacks of dynamic scopes that are familiar from the TeX model. This release adds support for fluid bindings via \alloc, \put, \get in order to simulate this behavior of TeX where desired; a scope can be delimited using \scope{...}. This can be useful for providing flexible configurations to macros.
Tables of contents. Tables of contents are now rendered by the default forest.xsl stylesheet.
LaTeX output. LaTeX output with full bibliography support is now available; this feature is still experimental, and may not work properly with some constructs (e.g. code blocks, etc.).

Name resolution. The behavior of name resolution was highly erratic in the prior release, and this has been corrected by adopting the yuujinchou library. This change may break trees that depended on the prior behavior.

Namespaces and hierarchical identifiers. Identifiers in the Forester language are now hierarchical, with components delimited by /. Namespaces can be created using \namespace\foo{...}, outside which all exported identifiers are prefixed by foo/. Namespaces can be opened into the current scope using \open\foo.

mainmatter.p: Armaël Guéneau, Riley Shahar, and Jon Sterling contributed code to this major release. Thanks also to Masanori Ogino for feature requests that made it into this release.

This release adds some new features to the command line interface. The original behavior of the forester command is now obtained by calling forester build. Please browse forester --help for more information.

frontmatter.title: Create new trees from the command line

mainmatter.p: A new command has been added in Forester 2.3 to simplify the creation of new trees with the correct base-36 identifier. If your personal namespace prefix is xxx, you may run the following command to create a new tree in the trees directory:
If the last tree in this directory was xxx-0039, a new tree will be generated at xxx-003A. The new tree will be populated with a correct \date declaration.

frontmatter.title: Tree title completion for tool support

mainmatter.p: A new forester complete command has been added to search for trees by their title; the purpose of this command is to facilitate external tool support, such as this proof-of-concept completion engine for neovim. Please browse forester complete --help for more information about this command.
Current limitations: the command is currently case sensitive and only searches prefixes; in the future, it would be desirable to support more flexible fuzzy-search.
Thanks to Riley Shahar for implementing this feature.

When Forester encounters an undefined function, it automatically passes through to the XML output. For instance, if the function \myfun is not defined, then the Forester code \myfun{hello} will render to the XML code <myfun>hello</myfun>. The Forester 2.3 extends this fall-through behavior to support XML attributes.
In particular, if you write \myfun{key1}{value1}{key2}{value2}{hello}, Forester will render the following XML code:
Thanks to Masanori Ogino for suggesting this feature, and welcome to the Forester community!.
In addition to the major changes above, the following minor changes are included in this release:

ol.li: A title attribute attribute has been added to the XML output of internal links, in order to facilitate tooltips and other affordances in rendered forests. Thanks to Armaël Guéneau for implementing this feature.
Subdirectories of input directories will now be traversed automatically; note that the tree address model remains flat, and subdirectories are present only for convenience.
Added a nicer command line interface with --help documentation.
I have migrated much of the system code to use the experimental Eio library for improved portability.
The example forest has been removed from the main repository, and moved into a separate template repository.
